title: async/await basics
successMessage: Same result, different style. async/await wins on readability — you can add variables, conditions, loops. Promise chains get unwieldy with branching logic.

title: await is just waiting
instruction:
`await` pauses the async function — the rest of the program keeps running.

This is the key insight: `await` doesn't block the whole program, just the async function it's in.

Run this and note the order.
successMessage: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — in that order. Step 3 runs BEFORE step 4 even though it comes after the await. The event loop kept going while the 100ms timer was running.

title: Error handling with try/catch
instruction:
With async/await, you use regular `try/catch` for errors — much cleaner than `.catch()` chains.

Await wraps the rejected promise rejection into a thrown error.
successMessage: try/catch with async/await — reviews never ran because orders threw. Errors jump to the nearest catch, exactly like synchronous code. This is why async/await is so readable.

title: Sequential vs parallel — critical difference!
instruction:
This is the most important async/await performance trap.

**Sequential** (slow): each await finishes before the next starts
**Parallel** (fast): start all at once, await all results together

Run both and see the time difference.
successMessage: Sequential: 300ms. Parallel: 100ms. NEVER chain awaits when the requests are independent — use Promise.all. This is the most impactful async optimisation.

title: Async functions always return Promises
instruction:
An `async` function ALWAYS returns a Promise — even if you return a plain value.

This means you can `.then()` on any async function call.
successMessage: async always wraps the return value in a Promise. This means async functions compose naturally — you can await any async function, nest them, pass them around.

title: Async iteration — for await...of
instruction:
`for await...of` loops over async iterables — like paginated API responses or file streams.

Simulate processing a stream of data one chunk at a time.
successMessage: for await...of processes each value as it arrives. This is how you handle streams, paginated APIs, and large datasets without loading everything into memory first.

title: Async error propagation
instruction:
Errors in async functions propagate naturally through the await chain.

A best practice: wrap the top-level async call in try/catch so errors don't go unhandled.
successMessage: Errors propagate through the entire async chain. step3 awaited step2, which awaited step1 — the error bubbled all the way up. Catch it at the highest level.

title: Build an async data fetcher with retry
instruction:
Final challenge — no starter code.

Build `fetchWithRetry(fn, options)` where options is `{ maxRetries: 3, delayMs: 100, timeout: 1000 }`.

- Wraps a Promise-returning function with retry logic
- Uses exponential backoff: delay doubles each retry (100ms, 200ms, 400ms)
- Times out the whole operation after `timeout` ms
- Logs each attempt

Test with a function that fails twice then succeeds.
hint: Use Promise.race with a timeout promise. Double delay each attempt.

codeChecks.message: Use await for the delay between retries.
Double the delay for exponential backoff.

successMessage: Retry + exponential backoff + timeout — the standard pattern for production API calls. Chapter 18 done — async/await is now in your toolbox!
